title: Regime-local beta calibration plus logit stacking
summary: Calibration papers show that a single global sigmoid can be too rigid, while beta calibration is often a stronger binary post-hoc map.
rationale: Calibration papers show that a single global sigmoid can be too rigid, while beta calibration is often a stronger binary post-hoc map. ROC AUC itself is rank-based, so calibration will not rescue a single model’s ordering, but regime-local calibrated logits can materially help stacking and blend stability when experts are mis-scaled differently across dry/wet, race phase, or season.
implementation_hint: Fit OOF beta calibrators separately for a few coarse regimes such as dry/wet x early/mid/late or by season, with fallback to global calibration when counts are small. Stack calibrated logits from XGB, CatBoost, and any new expert using a tiny regularized meta-model; select by blocked/grouped OOF only.
expected_effect: Small direct CV gain, but useful if current blends are limited by misaligned score scales rather than missing signal.
risk: Because calibration is monotone, standalone AUC may not move at all; over-segmented local calibrators can overfit and make the stack less stable.
